name: Performance Benchmarks and pprof Profiling
description: Write benchmarks, generate CPU and memory profiles, and analyze performance bottlenecks with pprof.

steps.title: Write a benchmark function
steps.description: Create a benchmark test file following Go naming conventions.

steps.title: Run benchmarks
steps.description: Execute benchmarks with memory allocation statistics.
steps.tip: Use -count=5 for statistically meaningful results. Use benchstat to compare multiple runs.

steps.title: Generate a CPU profile
steps.description: Run benchmarks and produce a CPU profile for analysis.

steps.title: Analyze the CPU profile interactively
steps.description: Open pprof to explore hotspots in the CPU profile.
steps.tip: The web UI shows flame graphs, call graphs, and source-level annotations. Focus on the widest bars in the flame graph.

steps.title: Generate and analyze memory profile
steps.description: Profile heap allocations to find memory-intensive code paths.
steps.command: go test -bench=BenchmarkProcess -memprofile=mem.prof ./... && go tool pprof -http=:6060 mem.prof
steps.warning: Memory profiles show allocations, not current memory usage. Look for high alloc_objects to find GC pressure.

steps.title: Compare benchmarks with benchstat
steps.description: Use benchstat to compare two benchmark runs and detect regressions.
steps.command: go test -bench=. -count=10 ./... > old.txt && go test -bench=. -count=10 ./... > new.txt && benchstat old.txt new.txt
steps.tip: Install benchstat with: go install golang.org/x/perf/cmd/benchstat@latest

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
# Go Benchmark and Profiling Workflow
set -euo pipefail

PACKAGE=${1:-"./..."}

echo "=== Running Benchmarks ==="
go test -bench=. -benchmem -count=3 "$PACKAGE" | tee bench.txt

echo ""
echo "=== Generating CPU Profile ==="
go test -bench=. -cpuprofile=cpu.prof -benchtime=5s "$PACKAGE"

echo ""
echo "=== Generating Memory Profile ==="
go test -bench=. -memprofile=mem.prof "$PACKAGE"

echo ""
echo "=== Top CPU Consumers ==="
go tool pprof -top cpu.prof 2>/dev/null | head -20

echo ""
echo "=== Top Memory Allocators ==="
go tool pprof -top mem.prof 2>/dev/null | head -20

echo ""
echo "=== Profiling Commands ==="
echo "Interactive CPU:  go tool pprof -http=:6060 cpu.prof"
echo "Interactive Mem:  go tool pprof -http=:6060 mem.prof"
echo "Compare runs:     benchstat old.txt new.txt"
echo "Trace:            go test -trace=trace.out && go tool trace trace.out"

faq.question: What does b.N mean in benchmarks?
faq.answer: b.N is the number of iterations the benchmark framework chooses. Go automatically adjusts it to run the benchmark long enough for a stable measurement. Do not set it manually.

faq.question: How do I profile a running HTTP server instead of benchmarks?
faq.answer: Import net/http/pprof in your server code. Then access profiles at /debug/pprof/. Use 'go tool pprof http://localhost:6060/debug/pprof/profile?seconds=30' to capture a 30-second CPU profile.

faq.question: What is the difference between -benchtime and -count?
faq.answer: -benchtime sets how long each individual benchmark runs (default 1s). -count sets how many times to repeat the entire benchmark suite. Use -count for statistical significance.
